notes.note: The abbreviated salutation: "Cicero to Memmius, greetings" (salutem dicit). C. Memmius is the same exiled politician-poet addressed in 13.1; he was now living in Athens where he had acquired property.

notes.anchor: C. Avianius Evander, who lives in your shrine-house
notes.note: C. Avianius Evander was a Greek sculptor and art-restorer living at Rome and Athens; he worked on famous statues and is known to have restored Praxiteles' work. Here he is lodging in Memmius's Athens property, which apparently had a small shrine (sacrarium) on the premises.

Under Roman social custom, a freed slave or dependent craftsman owed loyalty to his patron, who in turn was expected to advocate for him. This M. Aemilius was Evander's patron and a personal friend of Cicero's, making Cicero doubly motivated to intervene.

The Kalends (first day) of July was a common Roman lease-renewal date; Evander faced eviction on this date because Memmius's property was apparently tied up in ongoing construction projects that forced a change of tenancy.
